narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 38, you've clearly put in strong effort to build a competitive profile for law school, including planning to attend full-time and coming from a middle-income family background. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted a 68.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection—schools use this to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 1, also well below average, reinforced this worry as the second-most influential factor, suggesting early struggles that the model sees as persistent risks. On a brighter note, your LSAT score stood out positively, above the average of about 37, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA exceeded the typical 3.21, providing another favorable nudge in the model's eyes. Your full-time enrollment plan and middle-income background had minimal sway, neither strongly helping nor hurting the outcome. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your test scores and GPA are strengths that align with or beat averages, but your low decile rankings in Years 1 and 3 deviate sharply from the norm and dominated the decision. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome early hurdles to excel. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps seeking tutoring or study strategies tailored to law school rigor, and reapply once you've strengthened those areas. We're rooting for you to turn this around and pursue your legal ambitions successfully.
